Lung Cancer — Nodule Evaluation, Fleischner Follow-up & TNM 9th Staging

⚱Mental map — incidental nodule to lung-cancer stage

One track: detect a nodule → characterize (size, solid vs subsolid, morphology) → estimate risk → pick a management lever (surveillance · PET · biopsy · resection) → if malignant, stage (TNM 9th) to drive treatment.

  • ≤8 mm solid / subsolid incidental nodule → Fleischner 2017 surveillance schedule (recommender below).
  • >8 mm solid indeterminate → estimate probability of malignancy (Mayo) → <5% surveil · 5–65% PET then decide · >65% biopsy/resection.
  • Confirmed malignancy → assign T, N, M and read the 9th-edition stage group → stage-appropriate therapy.

Scope: incidental nodules in adults ≥35 y. Excludes lung-cancer screening (use Lung-RADS), immunosuppressed patients, and known primary cancer. Educational reference — not a treatment protocol.

1Nodule workup — the ACCP framework

Evaluation of the individual pulmonary nodule (ACCP/CHEST, 3rd ed). First fork is size; second is estimated probability of malignancy.

SituationSuggested approach
Solid ≤8 mmRisk-based CT surveillance per Fleischner 2017 (interval by size + risk). Biopsy/PET low-yield at this size.
Solid >8 mmEstimate pretest probability (judgment or a validated model), then: very low (<5%) → CT surveillance; low–moderate (5–65%) → PET/CT to refine, then surveil vs nonsurgical biopsy; high (>65%) → nonsurgical biopsy and/or surgical resection (with staging).
PET/CT roleBest for solid nodules >8 mm; also aids pretreatment staging. Less reliable for subsolid/ground-glass and lesions <8 mm (false negatives).
Subsolid nodulesManaged by Fleischner 2017 independent of the usual smoking-risk categories; persistence and a growing solid component drive escalation.
Tissue samplingTransthoracic needle biopsy (limited for very small / ground-glass lesions), navigational bronchoscopy + EBUS nodal sampling, or minimally invasive surgical biopsy/resection — choose in multidisciplinary discussion.

%Malignancy probability calculator Mayo model

Solitary indeterminate solid nodule. Mayo Clinic (Swensen) model as printed in the ACCP nodule guideline — six independent predictors.

Model: p = ex/(1+ex), x = −6.8272 + 0.0391·age + 0.7917·smoke + 1.3388·cancer + 0.1274·diameter + 1.0407·spiculation + 0.7838·upper-lobe. For solitary solid nodules; not validated for subsolid/ground-glass or screening-detected nodules. Bands per ACCP. Education only.

2Fleischner risk stratification — low vs high

Fleischner collapses the ACCP intermediate (5–65%) and high (>65%) categories into one high-risk group. Size and morphology dominate; add clinical factors.

Lower-risk features

  • Younger age, minimal/no smoking
  • Smaller size, smooth/regular margins
  • Non–upper-lobe location
  • Benign calcification or fat; perifissural/lymphoid morphology

Higher-risk features

  • Older age; heavy smoking (≥30 pack-years, quit <15 y)
  • Larger size; irregular or spiculated margins
  • Upper-lobe location
  • Emphysema or pulmonary fibrosis; asbestos/radon/uranium exposure; family history

Subsolid-nodule recommendations are applied independent of these smoking-risk categories.

▶Fleischner 2017 follow-up recommender

Incidental nodule, adult ≥35 y (not screening/immunosuppressed/known cancer). Choose type, size, and — for solid nodules — risk.

Thresholds from Fleischner 2017 (MacMahon et al). For multiple nodules, use the most suspicious as guide. Part-solid nodules generally not defined until ≥6 mm; a solid component ≥6 mm is highly suspicious. Confirm against the source table before clinical use.

3Fleischner 2017 management tables

The full recommendation grid — the recommender reads directly from these.

A · Solid nodules

Type<6 mm (<100 mm³)6–8 mm (100–250 mm³)>8 mm (>250 mm³)
Single
Low riskNo routine follow-upCT at 6–12 mo, then consider CT at 18–24 moConsider CT at 3 mo, PET/CT, or tissue sampling
High riskOptional CT at 12 moCT at 6–12 mo, then CT at 18–24 moConsider CT at 3 mo, PET/CT, or tissue sampling
Multiple
Low riskNo routine follow-upCT at 3–6 mo, then consider CT at 18–24 moCT at 3–6 mo, then consider CT at 18–24 mo
High riskOptional CT at 12 moCT at 3–6 mo, then at 18–24 moCT at 3–6 mo, then at 18–24 mo

B · Subsolid nodules

Type<6 mm (<100 mm³)≥6 mm (≥100 mm³)
Ground-glass, singleNo routine follow-upCT at 6–12 mo to confirm persistence, then CT every 2 y until 5 y
Part-solid, singleNo routine follow-upCT at 3–6 mo to confirm persistence; if unchanged and solid component <6 mm, annual CT for 5 y
Subsolid, multipleCT at 3–6 mo; if stable, consider CT at 2 and 4 yCT at 3–6 mo; subsequent management by most suspicious nodule(s)

Does not apply to screening, immunosuppression, or known primary cancer. Dimensions = average of long and short axes, rounded to nearest mm.

◎Measurement & characterization pearls

Measure it right

  • Use thin sections (≤1.5 mm, typically 1 mm); report the average of long- and short-axis diameters, rounded to nearest mm.
  • For subsolid nodules, measure the whole lesion and the solid component separately — the solid part drives risk.
  • Coronal/sagittal reconstructions help distinguish nodules from scars/vessels.

Morphology that lowers concern

  • Benign calcification (central, laminated, popcorn) or macroscopic fat (hamartoma).
  • Perifissural / juxtapleural triangular or lentiform nodules (intrapulmonary lymph nodes).
  • Persistent smooth linear/scar-like opacities.

▦Fleischner 2017 figure atlas

The paper's illustrative cases — measurement technique, benign vs suspicious morphology, subsolid nodules, and growth. Captions kept verbatim; click any image to zoom.

ΔTNM 9th edition — what changed

Proposed 9th-edition classification (Detterbeck et al; IASLC). T and M size cut-points are unchanged from the 8th edition; changes are in N and M1c subdivision and the resulting stage-group re-assignments.

  • N2 split → N2a (single ipsilateral mediastinal/subcarinal station) vs N2b (multiple N2 stations).
  • M1c split → M1c1 (multiple mets, single organ system) vs M1c2 (multiple organ systems).
  • Stage re-assignments concentrate in IIA, IIB, IIIA, IIIB — e.g. T1N1 moves IIB → IIA.
  • Stage subgroups validated for both clinical (c) and pathologic (p) staging.

4T · N · M definitions (9th edition)

TDefinition
TisCarcinoma in situ (squamous or adenocarcinoma)
T1miMinimally invasive adenocarcinoma (≤3 cm, lepidic, ≤5 mm invasion)
T1a / T1b / T1c≤1 cm / >1–2 cm / >2–3 cm
T2a / T2b>3–4 cm / >4–5 cm — or visceral pleura invasion, main-bronchus involvement (not carina), or atelectasis/obstructive pneumonitis to the hilum
T3>5–7 cm — or parietal pleura/chest wall, phrenic nerve, pericardium invasion, or separate nodule in the same lobe
T4>7 cm — or mediastinum, diaphragm, heart/great vessels, trachea/carina, esophagus, vertebra invasion, or separate nodule in a different ipsilateral lobe
NDefinition
N0No regional nodal metastasis
N1Ipsilateral peribronchial / hilar nodes
N2a 9thSingle ipsilateral mediastinal/subcarinal station
N2b 9thMultiple ipsilateral mediastinal/subcarinal stations
N3Contralateral mediastinal/hilar, or any scalene/supraclavicular node
MDefinition
M0No distant metastasis
M1aMalignant pleural/pericardial effusion or nodules; contralateral-lobe nodule
M1bSingle extrathoracic metastasis
M1c1 9thMultiple extrathoracic mets, single organ system
M1c2 9thMultiple extrathoracic mets, multiple organ systems

ΣTNM 9th stage-group calculator

Select T, N, and M to read the proposed 9th-edition stage group.

Stage map transcribed from the 9th-edition category and grid tables (Figs 2–3, Detterbeck et al). T1a/b/c with N0M0 → IA1/IA2/IA3. Any M1a/M1b → IVA; M1c1/M1c2 → IVB. Proposed classification — confirm against the official IASLC/UICC tables before clinical use.

5Stage-group grid (9th edition, M0)

T \ NN0N1N2aN2bN3
T1a–cIA1–3IIAIIBIIIAIIIB
T2aIBIIBIIIAIIIBIIIB
T2bIIAIIBIIIAIIIBIIIB
T3IIBIIIAIIIAIIIBIIIC
T4IIIAIIIAIIIBIIIBIIIC

M1a or M1b → Stage IVA (any T, N). M1c1 or M1c2 → Stage IVB. Tis N0M0 → Stage 0.

📖Sources

  • MacMahon H, Naidich DP, Goo JM, et al. Guidelines for Management of Incidental Pulmonary Nodules Detected on CT Images: From the Fleischner Society 2017. Radiology 2017;284(1):228–243. doi:10.1148/radiol.2017161659
  • Gould MK, Donington J, Lynch WR, et al. Evaluation of Individuals With Pulmonary Nodules: Diagnosis and Management of Lung Cancer, 3rd ed: ACCP Guidelines. Chest 2013;143(5 Suppl):e93S–e120S.
  • Detterbeck FC, Woodard GA, Bader AS, et al. The Proposed Ninth Edition TNM Classification of Lung Cancer. Chest 2024;166(4). (Stage tables reprinted from Rami-Porta et al.)
  • Swensen SJ, et al. The probability of malignancy in solitary pulmonary nodules (Mayo model). Arch Intern Med 1997;157:849–855.
Personal study reference synthesizing three source articles (Fleischner 2017 nodule guidelines; ACCP 3rd-ed nodule evaluation; proposed 9th-edition TNM). Management thresholds, staging definitions, and the malignancy-probability equation are reproduced as factual data; narrative text is paraphrased; figure images and captions are reproduced from Fleischner 2017 for personal educational use only and remain © RSNA. Calculators implement published rules but may contain transcription errors — verify every recommendation and stage against the primary sources before clinical use. The TNM 9th edition is a proposed classification; confirm the finalized IASLC/UICC tables. Not a substitute for multidisciplinary judgment.
